critique.summary: The article claims that Cloudflare has flagged the domain archive.today as associated with command and control (C&C) or botnet activity and that it no longer resolves via Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS service. However, the provided content does not contain any substantive information or evidence supporting these claims.
mainPoints: archive.today no longer resolves via Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS resolver.
critique.truthfulness: The article content is insufficient to verify the claims made in the title. The text shown is a Cloudflare security verification page, which does not provide any evidence or confirmation that archive.today is flagged as malicious or that it is blocked on 1.1.1.2. Independent verification from Cloudflare's official threat intelligence or DNS filtering lists is necessary to confirm these claims. Without such evidence, the claims remain unsubstantiated.
considerations: archive.today is a popular web archiving service often used for preserving web pages.
Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS service is designed to block malware and adult content, but blocking decisions are subject to change and should be verified with official sources.
False positives in DNS-based blocking can occur, so confirmation from multiple sources is important.
The article does not provide any sources or data to support the claim.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Cloudflare flagging archive.today as a command and control (C&C) botnet and its implications, including alleged denial-of-service attacks originating from archive.today and Cloudflare's fluctuating DNS resolution policies. Users express concern over security and trust issues related to archive.today's behavior.

comments.author: winkelmann
comments.text:
"archive.today is currently categorized as: * CIPA Filter * Reference * Command and Control & Botnet * DNS Tunneling"

Ditto for their other domains like archive.is and archive.ph

Example DoH request:

$ curl -s "https://1.1.1.2/dns-query?name=archive.is&type=A" -H "accept: application/dns-json"

{"Status":0,"TC":false,"RD":true,"RA":true,"AD":false,"CD":false,"Question":[{"name":"archive.is","type":1}],"Answer":[{"name":"archive.is","type":1,"TTL":60,"data":"0.0.0.0"}],"Comment":["EDE(16): Censored"]}

---

Relevant HN discussions:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46843805 "Archive.today is directing a DDoS attack against my blog"

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47092006 "Wikipedia deprecates Archive.today, starts removing archive links"

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46624740 "Ask HN: Weird archive.today behavior?" - Post about the script used to execute the denial-of-service attack

Wikipedia page on deprecating and replacing archive.today links:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Archive.today_guidan...
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed evidence, including DNS query results and references to prior discussions and Wikipedia, supporting the claim about archive.today's classification.

comments.author: stuffoverflow
comments.text: Archive.today's attack on https://gyrovague.com is still on-going btw. It started just over two months ago. Some IPs get through normally but for example finnish residential IPs get stuck on endless captchas. The JS snippet that starts spamming gyrovague appears after solving the first captcha.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a specific example of alleged malicious behavior from archive.today, adding context to the discussion, though it is anecdotal and less verifiable than DNS data.

comments.author: razingeden
comments.text:
Cloudflare dns has gone back and forth on whether it wants to resolve them since 2019. It’s taken that away and restored it again (intentionally? mistake?) at least four times.

The c&c/botnet designation would seem to be new though.
comments.analysis: This comment provides historical context on Cloudflare's DNS policies, which aligns with the known behavior of DNS providers reacting to security concerns.

comments.author: _moof
comments.text: Good. You don't get to use my computer for a DDoS. I don't care why the DDoS was happening. I wasn't asked, and that's a serious breach of trust.
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong personal stance on unauthorized use of resources for attacks, reflecting user sentiment but lacking technical detail.

comments.author: charcircuit
comments.text: When the heat dies down, hopefully this flag gets removed.
comments.analysis: A brief hopeful comment without evidence or technical insight, adding little substantive value to the discussion.
